Title: No 'happy ending' for Johor massage parlours in immigration raid
Article:
JOHOR BARU: An immigration crackdown on suspected vice activities at two massage parlours has led to the detention of 22 people in raids across the state capital.
Those held include 21 foreign nationals and a Malaysian man believed to be the outlets' manager.
Johor Immigration Department director Datuk Mohd Rusdi Mohd Darus said the arrests were made during a 3pm operation on April 9 under Ops Gegar, targeting two massage and reflexology centres.
The undocumented workers were suspected of offering illicit services, including so-called "happy ending" massages, with rates as low as RM80 per hour.
Ops Gegar is an enforcement drive combining intelligence gathering with targeted raids to tackle illegal foreign workers, abuse of passes and vice-related activities.
Rusdi said the operation followed sustained intelligence work and public tip-offs.
A total of 21 foreign nationals, aged between 18 and 40, were detained for suspected immigration offences. They comprise 19 Myanmar nationals, including 12 women, and two Chinese women, believed to be working as masseurs.
Investigations are under way to determine whether the outlets were operating as fronts for illegal activities.
All foreign detainees are being investigated under the Immigration Act 1959/63 and Immigration Regulations 1963.
They have been placed at the Setia Tropika Immigration Depot for further action, while seven individuals have been issued with summonses to assist in the investigation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US, Iran set for peace talks but doubts emerge over Lebanon, sanctions
Article:
ISLAMABAD: The US and Iran were to hold negotiations in the Pakistani capital Islamabad on Saturday seeking to end their six-week-old war, although Teheran threw the talks into doubt by saying they could not begin without commitments on Lebanon and sanctions.
The US delegation, led by Vice President JD Vance and including President Donald Trump's special envoy Steve Witkoff and son-in-law Jared Kushner, was on its way to Islamabad after a refuelling stop in Paris.
The Iranian delegation, led by parliamentary Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf and Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi, arrived on Friday.
IRAN HAS 'NO CARDS', TRUMP SAYS
Ghalibaf said on X that Washington had previously agreed to unblock Iranian assets and to a ceasefire in Lebanon, where Israeli attacks on Iran-backed Hizbollah fighters have killed nearly 2,000 people since the start of the fighting in March. He said talks would not start until those pledges were fulfilled.
Israel and the US have said the Lebanon campaign is not part of the Iran-US ceasefire while Teheran insists it is.
Ghalibaf said separately that Iran was ready to reach a deal if Washington offered what he described as a genuine agreement and granted Iran its rights, Iranian state media reported.
The White House did not immediately comment on the Iranian demands, but Trump posted on social media that the only reason the Iranians were alive was to negotiate a deal.
"The Iranians don't seem to realise they have no cards, other than a short-term extortion of the world by using international waterways. The only reason they are alive today is to negotiate!" he said.
Vance, speaking as he headed to Pakistan, said he expected a positive outcome but added: "If they're going to try to play us, then they're going to find the negotiating team is not that receptive."
Preliminary discussions have been separately held by Pakistani officials with advance teams from both sides, sources in Islamabad said.
Iran's semi-official Tasnim News Agency said these included 70 members from Teheran, including technical specialists in economic, security and political fields as well as media personnel and support staff. About 100 members of an advance US team were in the city, a Pakistani government source said.
"We're very positive," said another Pakistani source close to the discussions.
Asked if talks would end on Saturday, the source said: "Too early to say. They have instructions to close a deal or walk away. Hence not in a rush. These talks are not on the clock."
Islamabad was under an unprecedented lockdown ahead of the talks with thousands of paramilitary personnel and army troops on the streets.
"We have deployed multi-layer security for this event, which is based on coordination, intelligence and constant monitoring for zero disruption and full control," Pakistan's junior interior minister Talal Chaudhry told Reuters.
Trump announced a two-week ceasefire in the war on Tuesday, which has halted US and Israeli airstrikes on Iran.
But it has not ended Iran's blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, which has caused the biggest-ever disruption to global energy supplies, or calmed the parallel war between Israel and Iran-backed Hizbollah in Lebanon.
FIGHTING CONTINUES IN LEBANON
The Israeli ambassador to the US, Yechiel Leiter, and his Lebanese counterpart, Nada Hamadeh Moawad, will hold talks in Washington on Tuesday, Israeli and Lebanese officials said, amid the conflicting accounts on what those talks would cover.
Lebanon's presidency said the two had held a phone call on Friday and agreed to discuss announcing a ceasefire and setting a start date for bilateral talks under US mediation.
But Israel's embassy in Washington said the talks would constitute the start of "formal peace negotiations" and that Israel had refused to discuss a ceasefire with Hizbollah.
Israeli attacks continued across southern Lebanon on Friday. One strike on a government building in the city of Nabatieh killed 13 members of Lebanon's state security forces, President Joseph Aoun said in a statement.
Hizbollah said in a statement on its Telegram channel that it fired rocket salvos at northern Israeli towns in response.
Hours after the ceasefire was announced, Israel launched the biggest attack of the war, killing more than 350 people in surprise strikes on heavily populated areas, Lebanese authorities said.
Teheran's agenda at the talks also includes demands for major new concessions, including the end of sanctions that crippled its economy for years, and acknowledgement of its authority over the Strait of Hormuz, where it aims to collect transit fees and control access in what would amount to a huge shift in regional power.
Iran's ships were sailing through the strait unimpeded on Friday, while those of other countries remained hemmed inside.
Disruption to energy supplies has fed inflation and slowed the global economy, with an impact expected to last for months even if negotiators succeed in reopening the strait.
The hard line taken by Iran's leaders ahead of the negotiations followed a defiant message from its new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei on Thursday.
Khamenei, yet to be seen in public since taking over from his father, who was killed on the war's first day, said Iran would demand compensation for all wartime damage.
"We will certainly not leave unpunished the criminal aggressors who attacked our country," he said.
Although Trump has declared victory and degraded Iran's military capabilities, the war has not achieved many of the aims he set out at the start: to deprive Iran of the ability to strike its neighbours, dismantle its nuclear programme, and make it easier for its people to overthrow their government.
Iran still possesses missiles and drones capable of hitting its neighbours and a stockpile of more than 400 kg (900 pounds) of uranium enriched near the level needed to make a bomb. Its clerical rulers, who faced a popular uprising just months ago, withstood the onslaught with no sign of organised opposition.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Three Malaysians arrested in Thailand over 97kg meth haul
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Three Malaysian men in their 20s have been arrested in northeastern Thailand with 97kg of crystal methamphetamine.
Bukit Aman Narcotic Crime Investigation Department director Datuk Hussein Omar Khan said Thai police shared details of the arrests through the Narcotics Nucleus Secretariat at the border.
The suspects, all from Sarawak, were allegedly recruited by an international syndicate to transport the drugs from Laos into Thailand by rail.
(MORE TO COME)
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police destroy four fish bombs found near Malaysia-Thailand border
Article:
TANAH MERAH: Four improvised explosive devices (IEDs), believed to be fish bombs, were destroyed after being discovered near the Malaysia-Thailand border yesterday.
General Operations Force Southeast Brigade commander Senior Assistant Commissioner Ahmad Radzi Hussain said the bombs were found about 300m from Pos Nurut and just 15m from the border, following a tip-off from the public.
He said a Strike Force team, assisted by personnel from GOF's Ninth Battalion, went to Pos Nurut to verify the discovery about 2.30pm.
"Checks led to the discovery of four objects resembling fish bombs, believed to have been left at the base of an oil palm tree," he said.
Radzi said, about 6pm, the bomb disposal unit from the Kelantan police headquarters, assisted by forensic personnel, conducted an inspection and confirmed that all four devices were homemade IEDs commonly used for illegal fishing activities.
"The bomb disposal unit successfully carried out a controlled destruction of all the devices at the scene later," he said.
He added that investigations into the motive behind the placement of the devices at the base of the oil palm tree were ongoing.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Students' 30-second fight outside school goes viral
Article:
JOHOR BARU: A 30-second video that captured a fight between two groups of secondary school students outside a school here has gone viral.
The clip, which began circulating on social media yesterday, is believed to have been recorded outside a secondary school in Taman Selesa Jaya after school hours.
In the footage, students mostly boys are seen trading punches in full view of bystanders, including other students and crowd of onlookers gathers around them.
They were seen wearing T-shirts bearing the school's credentials.
Some can be heard shouting while others appear to record the altercation, as the scuffle escalates briefly before the video cuts off.
The incident has triggered a wave of reactions online, with many calling for stricter supervision and immediate intervention by authorities.
The New Straits Times has contacted the police and state Education and Information Committee chairman Aznan Tamin for comments.
No official statement has been released by the school or authorities as of press time.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Promoter arrested over RM1,500 toy theft at Mid Valley
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have arrested a 40-year-old promoter for allegedly stealing toys worth more than RM1,500 from a toy shop in Mid Valley Megamall.
Brickfields police chief Assistant Commissioner Hoo Chang Hook said the suspect was detained following a report lodged by the shop's sales manager on Friday.
"The complainant discovered discrepancies during a stock check on April 8 and found several items missing from the display counter. Further checks revealed empty boxes concealed in the store area," he said in a statement.
Hoo said closed-circuit television (CCTV) footage showed the suspect, a promoter for a supplier company, removing the items between 6.45pm and 7.50pm on April 7.
"The suspect had entered the premises to arrange stock before placing the items into a sling bag he brought along," he said.
The stolen items included various collectables, including Wolverine, Iron Man, Deadpool, and several Spider-Man variants, worth RM1,529.10.
All the stolen items have been recovered.
Initial investigations revealed the suspect had no prior criminal record and claimed the toys were for his child.
The case is being investigated under Section 380 of the Penal Code for theft in a building, which carries a jail term of up to 10 years and a fine upon conviction.
The suspect has been remanded for three days until April 13.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt to seek alternative materials to keep drug prices from soaring
Article:
JOHOR BARU: The government will implement intervention measures and explore alternative raw materials to address the rising costs of medicines and medical devices caused by the global energy crisis.
Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir said the government is focusing on ensuring supply availability and finding alternatives to reduce dependence on existing sources.
He said immediate steps are necessary to ensure the supply of medicines and health devices remains stable and to avoid disruptions to the national healthcare system.
For example, he said, in the manufacturing of medical devices which previously relied only on resin, the use of polymer-based materials as an alternative can be explored.
However, he added that this requires immediate follow-up action from the government, including the recognition and certification of such material.
Speaking to reporters after officiating the SMK Taman Pelangi Parent-Teacher Association meeting, Akmal Nasrullah said these measures must be implemented promptly to prepare for potential supply pressures and price hikes expected around June and July.
"That is why when we say we need to be ready for June and July, the intervention must be made now so that we do not face supply disruptions in the future," he said.
He added that the government acknowledged that most goods, including medicines and medical devices, depend on fuel as a primary input, leading to increased costs when energy prices rise.
"When fuel is an input element, it will definitely affect production costs and subsequently the price of those goods"
Accordingly, Akmal Nasrullah said the government has directed implementing agencies to conduct in-depth studies on supply availability and arrange immediate intervention steps to ensure the supply chain remains stable.
Yesterday, Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said the ministry was monitoring price increases of up to 40 per cent for medicines and health devices due to the global energy crisis and supply chain disruptions. (https://www.nst.com.my/news/nation/2026/04/1414620/medicine-supply-stable-stocks-sufficient-until-end-june-says-dzulkefly)
He had said medicine prices are estimated to rise between 30 and 40 per cent, while some medical devices have seen increases of up to 100 per cent.
He had also said these cost increases began before March when industry players delayed supplies, causing prices to rise earlier in the market.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Anwar orders immediate assistance for Beluran school using tents as classrooms
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has taken the plight of students at a school in Beluran to heart, directing the authorities to immediately assist them.
Concerned with the fact that these students are forced to take lessons in tents, the prime minister ordered the Education Ministry (MoE) and the Implementation Coordination Unit of the Prime Minister's Department to solve the issue.
"Matters like these are deeply troubling... the comfort and welfare of children in their pursuit of knowledge must be given top priority, and immediate action must be carried out," he said in a social media posting.
Anwar included a link to a news article published by the Borneo Post, in which Likas assemblymen Tham Yun Fook had highlighted the plight of SK Balaban Jaya.
Tham had called on both the state and federal Education ministries to urgently address the plight of students there who are reportedly attending classes in temporary and unsuitable learning spaces such as tents and corridors of a mosque.
This, he said, was as the school does not yet have a complete permanent building.
Tham said he had received complaints from parents expressing concern over the condition, which they say is affecting the comfort and learning environment of the students.
He said he had been informed that MoE has already allocated approximately RM2 million for the construction of a permanent school building, but the project is expected to take time due to tender processes and construction timelines, which could extend over several months to a year.
While acknowledging the allocation, Tham urged immediate interim measures to resolve the urgent needs of the students, saying more suitable temporary classrooms utilising, for instance, cabins should be made available.
Meanwhile, MoE said it was aware of and remained concerned about the issues of educational access and safety arising at SK Balaban Jaya.
It said the issue stemmed from damage to parts of the infrastructure caused by soil movement and slope instability, rendering the school unsafe.
It added that several engagement sessions had already been conducted by the Sabah Education Department with stakeholders, including parents and the local community, to find a resolution.
"Immediate actions to be taken include temporarily transferring students to SK Pekan Beluran and providing transportation to that school. This step is to ensure that students' learning is not affected and parents are not burdened.
"Upgrading works at SK Sungai Nangka will also be implemented immediately to meet the requirements for student dispersal while awaiting the new construction at SK Balaban Jaya," it said in a statement.
The ministry said it would ensure the educational needs of students at SK Balaban Jaya, as well all school communities, are safeguarded.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rapid Rail fined RM100,000 over 2021 LRT crash
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Rapid Rail Sdn Bhd has been fined RM100,000 for the 2021 Light Rail Transit (LRT) collision that left 65 passengers injured.
The Land Public Transport Agency (Apad) said the penalty follows investigation findings into maintenance shortcomings by the operator.
The incident occurred on May 24, 2021, between the KLCC and Kampung Baru stations.
Apad said Rapid Rail failed to ensure critical track components were in proper working condition.
"As a licensed operator, Rapid Rail failed to ensure the rail alignment was safe for operations, leading to the incident. This resulted in the inability to operate the train safely, causing the accident," Apad said in a statement.
The offence falls under Section 101(10) of the Land Public Transport Act, which mandates enforcement action against operators failing to meet safety requirements.
The agency said safety remains its highest priority, and the fine serves as a reminder to all rail operators to adhere to technical maintenance standards and ensure adequate personnel training.
"The decision to impose the compound also takes into account public interest and the principle of accountability in preserving the integrity of the nation's rail transport system," the agency said.
Former transport minister Datuk Seri Dr Wee Ka Siong
previously said
technical faults and a loss of communication between a train hostler and the operations control centre were among the factors behind the crash.
In 2023, eight victims filed a
civil lawsuit
against Rapid Rail and Prasarana alleging negligence.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Bentong camp assault: Soldier dies 12 days after assault by corporal
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A soldier who was allegedly assaulted by a junior non-commissioned officer at a camp in Bentong has died.
Private Muhammad Amirul Raziq Rosafindi died at a hospital in Temerloh, Pahang, today.
His father, Rosafindi Tambi Chik, confirmed the death when contacted.
"He breathed his last at 9.45am. He will be laid to rest at Pusara Damai Felda Sungai Kelamah in Gemas, Negeri Sembilan," he said.
The army is expected to issue a statement on the matter.
The assault was reported to have taken place
at the 25th Battalion Royal Malay Regiment (25 RMR) camp in Bentong on March 30.
It is believed to have stemmed from dissatisfaction over weapon store cleaning duties, with the suspect allegedly slapping and punching the victim in the face and abdomen.
On April 6, a corporal, Sukry Tasu, pleaded not guilty
at the Magistrate's Court to seven charges of assaulting fellow personnel last month.
He was charged under Section 325 of the Penal Code for causing grievous hurt, which carries a maximum sentence of seven years' imprisonment and a fine.
Sukry also faces six additional charges of injuring other personnel, aged between 19 and 22, at the same location later that day. These charges are framed under Section 323 of the Penal Code, which provides for up to one year's imprisonment, a fine of up to RM2,000, or both.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Chow Kit is like a heaven for us'
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The back alleys of Chow Kit might be dreaded by outsiders, but they have become a "heaven" for Azza, a place where she can raise her three children.
The young, single mother in her mid-30s with three children, has been raising her children there over the past 10 years.
She said she settled in Chow Kit after marrying her now former husband, who was a drug addict.
"The environment in Chow Kit is admittedly not suitable for building a family or raising children. But I was accepted and well-cared for by the community here.
"I have a 'sister' who has looked after me and struggled alongside me without judging my life.
"Maybe it's because they are also struggling. So, they understand more and never hesitate to help, whether it's in terms of money, food, or looking after the children.
"For example, my 'sister' is very close to me; she often shares whatever blessings she receives, whether it's money or the food she gets," she said.
Azza admitted that she has no permanent job or steady source of income.
"I rely heavily on friends, including my ex-husband, to survive in the alleys of Chow Kit," she said.
Azza's daily routine is almost the same every day: looking for work at nearby shops or restaurants so that she can keep an eye on her children.
"I don't apply for aid from the Welfare Department because I'm worried my children will be taken away from my care.
"I only rely on zakat assistance. That is what I use to pay for a small rented room in a shophouse, with a rental rate of RM450 a month.
"That is where we eat, and that is where we sleep or rest. The room is indeed small and uncomfortable for the children. But that is all I can afford.
"If I were to rent elsewhere, I worry the children wouldn't be able to attend school because they go to Sekolah Bimbingan Jalinan Kasih near the zakat centre.
"Like it or not, I am forced to let my children play in the area around the shophouses on this street.
"The area around the shops here is open. People around here recognise my children if I am unable to accompany them.
"I ask friends or my 'sister' to help watch over the children if I am working," she said.
Azza added that she had once moved out and lived in other areas, but eventually returned to Chow Kit because she felt more accepted by the locals.
"I lived in Batu Caves for a short while, but the acceptance there was quite poor.
"I moved at the request of my family, who wanted me to have a 'normal' life outside of Chow Kit, but it didn't last long.
"'People looked down on us and there was a stigma against my children and me. So, I came back to Chow Kit because here, they never judge or think negatively.
"I feel that I can still survive here. Even the addicts or social workers never bother us," she said.
Azza said, although she has no income and has even run out of food supplies at times, she has never been involved in criminal activities or social ills.
"Even though I have no money, I have never gone as far as 'selling myself' or begging. I still try to find work and ask for help from NGOs that I know," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Diesel misappropriation syndicate using 4,500-litre tank lorry crippled
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Authorities have crippled a syndicate that used a lorry fitted with a modified 4,500-litre tank to misappropriate subsidised diesel for the industrial market.
The group used five Subsidised Diesel Control Scheme (SKDS) fleet cards belonging to various companies to refuel a five-tonne lorry at multiple petrol stations.
Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry enforcement officers uncovered the activity after trailing the suspects through six petrol stations along the Shah Alam Expressway (Kesas) before moving in.
During the raid, the syndicate had already pumped approximately 2,500 litres of diesel into a hidden tank equipped with a pumping system connected to the vehicle's original fuel tank.
The ministry's Kuala Lumpur enforcement officer Mohd Shahran Mohd Arshad said two men in their 20s, suspected to be drivers for the syndicate, were detained.
"Based on intelligence, their modus operandi involved using various fleet cards to fill up at stations around the Klang Valley before transporting the fuel to a storage facility," he said.
Shahran said preliminary investigations suggested the suspects abused the fleet cards to purchase between 100 and 250 litres of subsidised diesel at each station.
He said the diesel was initially pumped into the lorry's original tank, then transferred to the larger container in the cargo hold via a modified piping system to avoid detection.
"The suspects acted as workers tasked with collecting fuel from stations linked to the fleet cards.
"Once the tank was full, it would be driven to a specific location where the syndicate would take over the vehicle. Interrogations revealed they were paid RM300 per trip," he said.
He added that the diesel purchased at subsidised rates was believed to be resold to industries at a higher price.
The raid comes amid reports that some petrol stations had run out of diesel amid the Middle East conflict.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Marine police seize diesel worth over RM1 million in Tawau
Article:
TAWAU: The Marine Police Force in Tawau seized 30,800 litres of diesel worth RM1.16 million and arrested a local man in a raid at a warehouse on Jalan Sin San here yesterday.
Sabah Marine Police Region 4 commander ACP Ahmad Amri Abd Rahman said the 5 pm raid was carried out based on PPM intelligence and a public tip-off, before trailing a lorry driven in a suspicious manner to the location.
"Inspections found that illegal diesel transfer activities were taking place, and also uncovered three lorries, three skid tanks and 10 drum barrels believed to have been used for the misappropriation of controlled goods," he said in a statement here today.
Ahmad Amri said the case is being investigated under the Control of Supplies Act 1961, and enforcement operations will continue to be intensified to combat the misappropriation and smuggling of controlled goods, while ensuring economic stability and national security are preserved. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Global equities remain primary driver of EPF returns
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The equity asset class, particularly overseas investments, remains the primary contributor to the Employees Provident Fund's investment returns.
EPF chief executive officer Ahmad Zulqarnain Onn said the strong performance was partly driven by the rapid growth of the technology and artificial intelligence sectors, particularly in the United States market.
"The equity asset class remains the primary contributor to EPF's investment returns, especially overseas equities which benefited from the rapid growth of the technology and AI sectors, particularly in the US.
"At the same time, the EPF equity portfolio is diversified across various geographies and sectors. Last year, markets such as Japan, China, Korea, and Europe also recorded good performances and contributed to the overall performance of the portfolio," he told Berita Harian.
Zulqarnain said, for the 2025 to 2026 outlook, EPF is adopting a cautious approach amid an increasingly uncertain global environment, particularly following developments in the oil and gas sector which have the potential to pressure inflation, energy costs, and market sentiment.
"Under these circumstances, EPF will continue to practice active risk management and maintain a diversified portfolio spanning various geographies and sectors to ensure investment resilience and sustainable returns for its members," he said.
He added that the global diversification strategy continues to be strengthened in line with the growth of the EPF's fund size.
"In 2025, global investments accounted for 38.3 per cent of the portfolio and generated RM39.9 billion, equivalent to 50.4 per cent of the total investment income," he said.
However, he stressed that EPF remains committed to supporting the domestic market, as more than 70 per cent of its annual investment allocation is still focused within the country.
He said overseas investments are essential to ensure diversification, sustainable returns, and risk reduction over the long term.
"As a retirement fund, every investment decision is centered on our fiduciary responsibility to protect and grow members' savings.
"Portfolio rebalancing, including repatriation (the return of proceeds) for liquidity purposes and risk adjustment, is implemented without compromising long-term strategy.
"This approach ensures that portfolio stability remains preserved while fulfilling the interests of EPF members in the long term," he said.
Meanwhile, Zulqarnain said EPF will continue to increase its investment allocation in Malaysian Government Securities (MGS) and Government Investment Issues (GII) to ensure long-term portfolio stability.
He said approximately 40 per cent of the annual projected net investment funds are allocated to these fixed-income instruments.
"In line with the growth of EPF's fund size, the annual investment allocation for MGS and GII has also increased to ensure stable returns and portfolio liquidity," he said.
He said investments in these instruments not only support portfolio stability but also contribute to the country's economic development.
At the same time, he added, EPF maintains an "overweight" approach toward global equities as part of its strategy to ensure optimum exposure to growth assets.
"Global equities serve as a growth component that balances investment positions in private markets which remain below target levels," he said.
He said this approach ensures the portfolio's risk-return profile remains consistent and in line with the EPF's Strategic Asset Allocation framework.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Foreigner in viral KLIA2 ruckus video pleads guilty to hurting security officer
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A foreigner seen in a
viral video
pushing a security officer in Kuala Lumpur International Airport Terminal 2 recently was arrested and charged with causing hurt and insulting behaviour.
The 33-year-old from Liberia pleaded guilty to the charges at the Sepang court on Tuesday.
KLIA police chief Assistant Commissioner M. Ravi said the incident occurred on April 5, after the man had missed his connecting flight to Hat Yai.
"Acting on information, the arrest was made by KLIA policemen about 10.30am at the KLIA2 flight transfer counter.
S
"Initial investigations showed that the suspect had missed his flight and had gotten into an argument with his wife over issues regarding the care of their child.
"During the incident, the suspect acted aggressively by hitting the glass of the counter, smashing the flight information board, throwing a drink bottle at the counter and injuring a security officer who was on duty at the time.
Ravi said the suspect pleaded guilty to a charge of voluntarily causing hurt under Section 323 of the Penal Code and a charge under Section 14 of the Minor Offences Act for insulting behaviour.
Under the first charge, he is liable to a jail term of up to a year and a fine of up to RM2,000, or both, while the second charge leads to a fine of up to RM100.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Woman trapped, injured in four-vehicle crash in Sungai Buloh
Article:
SUNGAI BULOH: A woman in her 30s was injured after becoming trapped in a four-vehicle pile-up near the Sungai Pelong police station here today.
The 8.15am accident involved a Perodua Bezza, a Perodua Myvi, a Mercedes-Benz and a Yamaha Y15 motorcycle.
Selangor Fire and Rescue Department assistant director of operations Ahmad Mukhlis Mukhtar said a six-man team from the Sungai Buloh fire station was dispatched to the scene after receiving an emergency call.
"The victim was found trapped in one of the vehicles with head injuries but was still conscious when rescuers arrived," he said.
Ahmad Mukhlis said firemen arrived at 8.22am and immediately began extrication efforts.
He added that an Emergency Medical Rescue Services (EMRS) unit was also deployed to the location, and work was still ongoing at the scene.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: USM students represent Malaysia at rocketry competition in US
Article:
NIBONG TEBAL: A team of 10 students from Universiti Sains Malaysia (USM) is set to take Malaysian innovation to new heights as they prepare to compete in the International Rocket Engineering Competition 2026 (IREC 2026) in Midland, Texas, United States from June 15 to 20.
Known as WAU Rocketry USM, the team will represent Malaysia alongside another team from Universiti Teknologi Malaysia (UTM) at one of the world's largest intercollegiate rocketry competitions, which bring together students from around the world to design, build and launch rockets.
In an exclusive interview with Bernama, the team leader P. Vymarleena Avriel, 23, said their APEX-1 rocket is designed to ascend 10,000 feet into the air, competing in the Commercial Off The Shelf (COTS) category.
Unlike large-scale launch vehicles developed by agencies such as National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) or SpaceX, she explained that the APEX-1 is a high-powered sounding rocket, a more compact system suitable for academic engineering competitions, yet still demanding rigorous design, simulation and testing.
"IREC 2026 is actually an engineering competition but it is for to launch rockets and we have students worldwide that participate in the competition. We launch a rocket and compete.
"I would say with my team and our current progress from the technical and engineering side, I am really confident and hoping to get at least a top five result but we can achieve even more," she told Bernama at the School of Aerospace Engineering, Engineering Campus, USM here.
As the only female member of the team, Vymarleena Avriel shared that the journey to IREC has taken nearly a year, beginning with a fully online application process that required detailed computer-aided design (CAD), simulations and technical reports.
She added that the competition involves multiple evaluation phases, including the Preliminary Design Report (PDR), Critical Design Review (CDR) and the Flight Readiness Review (FRR), before teams are cleared for launch.
The third-year Aerospace Engineering student said balancing academic commitments with the project has been one of the team's biggest challenges.
"Most of us are third-year students with packed schedules, so we work overnight and spend long hours in the lab, even during semester breaks, we stayed on campus to continue building the rocket," she added.
Beyond technical hurdles, she said financial constraints remain a significant obstacle, thus the team has actively reached out to sponsors, government agencies and industry players, securing partial funding while continuing efforts to cover remaining costs.
"As a student team, funding is one of our biggest challenges, not just for the rocket components but also for travel to the US," said Vymarleena  Avriel, who aspires to become an astronaut.
Due to regulatory restrictions in Malaysia, she also said the rocket will be assembled locally, disassembled for transport and reassembled in the US, where the motor classified under strict regulations and will be integrated before launch.
Meanwhile, the project supervisor Dr. Chang Wei Shyang commended the team's discipline and progress, noting that participation in competitions like IREC offers value beyond academics.
"We won seventh place in 2022 and competed again in 2023, but unfortunately unable to send teams after that due to financial issues and challenges to actually get the bright students into the team to continue.
"However, this new batch really have the fire to continue. I can see that the rocket is almost ready and consistently meet their deadlines without needing reminders, I think this speaks for itself how disciplined they are in this particular endeavor," he said.
Chang, a lecturer at USM's School of Aerospace Engineering, said the team's achievement showcases the capabilities of Malaysian students and demonstrates that the country can compete with leading universities globally, reflecting national progress in technology.
He also highlighted growing interest in aerospace among Malaysian students, with increasing enrolment and industry opportunities, including over 300 aerospace-related companies nationwide.
For the record, WAU Rocketry USM previously secured third place in the A4 International Category at the Teknofest 2025 Rocket Competition in Aksaray, Turkey, following the successful launch of their Hebat-3 rocket.
Six members including Vymarleena Avriel, participated in an international mobility programme at Hochschule Stralsund, Germany, gaining hands-on experience in hybrid rocket engine manufacturing.
Besides Vymarleena Avriel, the other team members are Lim Wiky, 25; Danish Irfan Saiful Nizam, 21; R.Krishna Baniel, 22;  Aizad Aizal Abdul Aziz, 22; K.Aditteya, 22, Muhammad Hafiz Hakimy Gazali, 22; Muhammad Farhat Mohd Sharipa, 22; M.Saravanan, 22 and S.Loganraj, 22. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kelantan migrant smuggling ring busted as repeat 'mastermind' rearrested
Article:
KOTA BARU: The immigration authorities say they have dismantled a migrant smuggling operation in Kelantan, arresting 27 people, including a suspected repeat offender described as the group's "mastermind".
The Immigration Department said, in a statement, the arrests were made during a special operation carried out around 6.30am on April 8 in and around Kota Baru, following intelligence gathered by its tactical unit and the Kelantan state enforcement team.
Two local men believed to be acting as "receivers" were initially detained, leading officers to raid a residential property suspected of being used as a holding house.
There, authorities arrested another local man, alleged to be the syndicate's leader, along with two more locals believed to be transporters.
A total of 22 foreign nationals were also detained. They comprised two Indian men, three Bangladeshi men, and 17 Myanmar nationals, including eight men and nine women. Those arrested were aged between 13 and 42.
"The syndicate had altered its methods, bringing undocumented migrants into the country via coastal routes before placing them in temporary accommodation to avoid detection.
"The migrants were then transported onwards to destinations in the Klang Valley," read the statement.
Authorities said transporters were paid RM100 per person, while receivers earned RM250 per migrant.
Investigators also seized several items, including passports from Bangladesh, India and Myanmar, mobile phones, and five vehicles.
The department said the alleged mastermind, linked to a group known as "Geng Borhan", had been detained previously but was believed to have resumed similar activities.
Five Malaysian men are being investigated under the Anti-Trafficking in Persons and Anti-Smuggling of Migrants Act 2007, while the foreign nationals are being held for suspected immigration offences.
Members of the public have been urged to report suspicious activities and not to harbour or assist individuals breaching immigration laws.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Manager held after Johor restaurant raid over alleged cooking oil abuse
Article:
JOHOR BARU: A restaurant in Taman Megah Ria, Masai, was raided on Thursday for allegedly misappropriating subsidised cooking oil, leading to the detention of its manager.
Johor Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry director Lilis Saslinda Pornomo said the 3pm operation was conducted in collaboration with the ministry's Melaka branch.
Initial investigations revealed that the manager, in his 40s, had been cutting open subsidised cooking oil packets and transferring the oil into bottles for use in the restaurant's operations.
He was detained to assist investigations, Lilis said in a statement.
"Enforcement officers also found scissors, funnels and bottles suspected to have been used."
Checks also found the restaurant had stored controlled goods, namely sugar, subsidised cooking oil and wheat flour, without a valid permit.
The ministry seized 120kg of coarse sugar, 200kg of wheat flour and 51kg of subsidised 1kg packet cooking oil.
The items and tools were confiscated for investigations under the Control of Supplies Act.
Upon conviction, offenders face a fine of up to RM1 million or imprisonment of up to three years, or both, for a first offence. Repeat offenders may be fined up to RM3 million or jailed up to five years, or both.
For companies, fines of up to RM2 million may be imposed for a first offence, and RM5 million for subsequent offences.
Subsidised cooking oil in 1kg packets is intended to ease the cost of living for lower-income households. The price gap between packet oil and bottled oil has long made the commodity a target for commercial diversion and repackaging.
The ministry urged the public to report the abuse of controlled goods via WhatsApp (019-848 8000), the e-Aduan portal, its call centre (1-800-886-800), or the Ez ADU KPDN mobile application.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Palestinians still unsafe six months after Gaza truce, says UN rights chief
Article:
BIRMINGHAM: Six months after a ceasefire took effect in Gaza, Palestinians across the territory remain unsafe as Israeli attacks continue on a near-daily basis, the United Nations' top human rights official said on Friday, Anadolu Ajansi (AA) reported.
UN High Commissioner for Human Rights Volker Turk said at least 738 Palestinians have been killed since the ceasefire began on Oct 10, 2025, including 32 deaths recorded since early April, according to figures from the Palestinian Ministry of Health.
"The unrelenting pattern of killings reflects continuing disregard for Palestinian lives, enabled by sweeping impunity," Turk said in a statement.
He noted a persistent pattern of strikes and shootings affecting civilians in homes, shelters and public spaces over the past 10 days, including incidents in tents housing displaced families, on streets and in vehicles, as well as at a medical facility and a classroom.
Among those killed were women, children, people with disabilities, a humanitarian contractor and a journalist, he said.
Turk cited the killing of a third-grade girl on April 9 when Israeli forces opened fire on a crowded tent encampment in Beit Lahiya in northern Gaza, where she was attending a makeshift class.
A day earlier, an Al Jazeera journalist was killed in Gaza City in a drone strike.
The Israeli military later said he had been a Hamas operative, a claim Turk said has been made in other cases involving journalists without independently verifiable evidence.
The UN human rights office has recorded 294 Palestinian journalists killed since Oct 7, 2023.
On April 6, Israeli forces shot at a vehicle carrying World Health Organisation workers, killing the driver.
Turk said 589 aid workers have been killed in Gaza since Oct 7, 2023, including 397 affiliated with the United Nations.
"The number of journalists and humanitarian personnel killed in Gaza is unprecedented," Turk said, adding that the violence has made both reporting and humanitarian work increasingly dangerous.
He also warned that Palestinians are being killed for approaching or being near a shifting Israeli military boundary in Gaza, known as the "yellow line," which he said is poorly marked.
Targeting civilians not directly participating in hostilities constitutes a war crime, regardless of their proximity to military positions, he added.
Turk said conditions have been worsened by restrictions on humanitarian aid, destruction of civilian infrastructure and continued instability within Gaza.
"Palestinians have no blueprint for survival. Whatever they do or don't do, wherever they go or don't go, there is no safety or protection afforded to them," he said.
He called on the international community to take concrete steps to ensure accountability and bring an end to violations of international law, saying that civilians must be allowed to begin rebuilding their lives after prolonged conflict. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tow truck operators to go on 3-day strike, demand diesel subsidies
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Tow truck services are expected to be disrupted for three days from tomorrow as operators will go on strike to urge the government to provide diesel subsidies.
A notice issued by insurance company Zurich Takaful said the Klang Valley is expected to be among the areas most affected, with the possibility that the strike will also impact other states across the peninsula.
It said operating hours for internal tow trucks will be extended to assist customers, with scheduled services being carried out based on service availability.
Additionally, it said customers may also arrange for their own tow trucks via third parties due to the service disruption, with reimbursements to be claimed according to the established terms and conditions.
Meanwhile, Persatuan Wakil Takaful Am Muslim (Perwatam) urged the government to immediately make improvements to the diesel subsidy mechanism, to ensure that the tow truck industry services do not collapse.
Perwatam president Datuk Khairi Anuar Ahmad, in a statement, said more realistic policy support is urgently needed to ensure the tow truck industry continues to operate.
"Perwatam emphasises that immediate action without compromise must be implemented by all parties.
"Any further delay is unacceptable and will only worsen the situation, as well as undermine public confidence in the insurance and takaful protection system in Malaysia," he said.
At the same time, Khairi said Perwatam is also urging all general insurance and takaful operating companies to immediately review and adjust the payment rates for tow assistance operators.
He said this is because existing charges do not reflect actual operating costs, and the failure to adjust tariffs undermines the ability of operators to function effectively, subsequently causing delays in assistance, service disruptions, and increased safety risks for customers on the ground.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Unplanned development among the causes of Klang Valley traffic congestion
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: An increase in the number of private vehicles is not the sole cause of the worsening traffic congestion in the Klang Valley.
It also stems from urban development that is not planned relative to regional mobility plans, as well as connectivity challenges within the public transport system.
Universiti Putra Malaysia Road Safety Research Centre head Professor Dr Law Teik Hua said this situation causes traffic flow to concentrate on specific routes, increasing pressure on the existing road networks.
He said many new developments are not comprehensively integrated with regional mobility plans, causing traffic flow to concentrate on specific routes and creating chronic traffic bottlenecks.
"The public transport network, although expanding, still faces challenges in terms of easy access from residential areas to (public transport) stations and from stations to final destinations, consequently encouraging the public to continue using private vehicles," he told Berita Harian.
Previously, Kuala Lumpur police chief Datuk Fadil Marsus had said more than three million vehicles entering the capital daily were identified as the primary cause of the extraordinary traffic congestion that occurred in early February.
He had said, based on monitoring and Integrated Transport Information System (ITIS) data, the influx of vehicles into Kuala Lumpur between Feb 1 and 12 showed a significant increase, exceeding three million vehicles daily.
Law said the imbalance between travel demand and infrastructural capacity further exacerbated the situation, as the growth of residential and commercial areas has not been aligned with the capacity of roads and transit systems.
He said traffic studies conducted by his team from March to September last year in the Klang Valley using the Automated Road Incident Detection System (ARIDS) recorded a congestion increase of up to 30 percent on the Damansara-Puchong Expressway (LDP) during that period.
He said the system is implemented on major highways and main road networks around Kuala Lumpur as well as major urban areas in the Klang Valley.
By leveraging data from Microsoft (Bing Maps), Google, and Baidu, it is capable of detecting accidents and analysing congestion levels in real-time, he added.
"Among the most alarming key findings is the 30 per cent increase in congestion levels within just six months on the LDP. This increase demonstrates that the rate of vehicle growth and the pressure on road capacity are occurring very rapidly.
"The patterns also show that peak hours are becoming longer; they are no longer just concentrated in the mornings and evenings, but are instead spilling over into non-office hours," he said.
Law said the impact of severe congestion is not only seen in travel times — which can increase by 20 to 50 per cent — but also carries psychological and economic implications.
"Repeated daily stress can contribute to chronic fatigue and impaired concentration, which in turn increases the risk of minor accidents.
"From an economic perspective, congestion contributes to fuel wastage, increased carbon emissions, and billions of ringgit in losses annually due to the loss of productive hours.
"On a macro level, it affects urban competitiveness and social well-being," he said.
Law said the traffic congestion in the Klang Valley continues even through festive seasons, including Aidilfitri.
He said, besides Kuala Lumpur, several other areas are also experiencing similar congestion, including Johor Baru, Penang, and rapidly developing cities in Selangor such as Shah Alam and Petaling Jaya.
"The contributing factors are almost identical: the increase in private vehicles, the concentration of economic and commercial activities, and the rise in 'balik kampung' travel."
Meanwhile, Universiti Sains Malaysia School of Civil Engineering Associate Professor Dr Nur Sabahiah Abdul Sukor said the high movement of logistics vehicles is also a cause of severe congestion in the Klang Valley.
She said logistics vehicles are also competing for road space to ensure that deliveries are not affected by the long holidays.
"The increase in the number of vehicles, poor land-use planning, and inefficient public transport access are also primary causes," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Lebanon, Israel to hold first meeting at US State Dept Tuesday
Article:
ISTANBUL: Lebanon and Israel agreed to hold their first meeting on Tuesday at the US State Department in Washington aimed at securing a ceasefire and launching direct talks, Anadolu Ajansi (AA) reported, citing the Lebanese presidency.
In a statement on X, the presidency said on Friday evening, that the move comes as part of an initiative by Lebanese President Joseph Aoun.
As part of the process and based on instructions from Aoun, Lebanon's Ambassador to Washington Nada Hamadeh Moawad held a phone call with Israeli Ambassador to Washington Yechiel Leiter, in what was described as "the first" contact between the two sides.
US Ambassador to Lebanon Michel Issa, who is currently in Washington, also participated in the call.
During the call, it was agreed to hold a first meeting next Tuesday at the US State Department headquarters to discuss the announcement of a ceasefire and the timing for the start of negotiations between Lebanon and Israel under US auspices. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Guan Eng proposes loans, tax moratorium for MSMEs amid Middle East crisis
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: DAP adviser Lim Guan Eng has proposed five short-term financial measures to rescue micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs) facing the global economic crisis.
The Bagan member of parliament's proposals include providing interest-free and unsecured loans of RM50,000 to 100,000 MSMEs, involving a RM5 billion allocation.
Lim said he submitted the proposals to Prime Minister and Pakatan Harapan chairman Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim at the coalition's Presidential Council meeting on Wednesday.
In a letter shared today, Lim highlighted concerns over the survival of 1.1 million MSMEs following the Middle East conflict, which has caused oil prices to soar.
The former finance minister said the government remains the primary hope for easing the cost burden of MSMEs and generating business to ensure they are not forced to close by the end of the year.
Lim also proposed a moratorium on interest payments for existing loans from financial institutions until year-end.
Another measure involves a moratorium on additional taxes and the expansion of compliance and regulatory costs.
This includes suspending the Sales and Service Tax (SST) increase, e-invoicing, and the 2 per cent Employees Provident Fund (EPF) contribution for foreign workers, which took effect late last year.
He suggested requiring all investors to source 50 per cent of their supplies from local manufacturers, unless the items are unavailable in the domestic market.
"The fifth proposal involves easing bureaucracy and strict enforcement during this crisis period," he said.
Lim said that beyond volatile raw material prices, supply chain disruptions, and surging shipping and logistics costs, MSMEs were also grappling with liquidity issues.
"Raw material suppliers are refusing to provide credit terms of one to three months, insisting on cash-based transactions instead," he said.
"Without raw materials, MSMEs cannot produce goods, complicating the finances of many businesses already forced to fund other soaring costs," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Beautician molested near MRT station, foreigner arrested
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A 20-year-old foreigner has been arrested for allegedly molesting a beautician near the Kuchai MRT station on Thursday night.
Brickfields police chief Assistant Commissioner Hoo Chang Hook said the victim, 22, was approached by the suspect, an unemployed Chinese national, who hugged her from behind without her consent.
The incident occurred along the walkway of the station while the victim was heading to the restroom.
Hoo said the suspect was arrested at 8.10pm the same day.
"He has been remanded until April 24 to assist in investigations," he said.
The case is being investigated under Section 354 of the Penal Code for outraging modesty, which carries a maximum 10-year prison sentence, a fine, whipping, or any combination of the three upon conviction.
"Police are also investigating the suspect under Section 6(1) of the Immigration Act for entering the country without valid travel documents.
In a separate incident, auxiliary policemen detained a 19-year-old foreigner less than 24 hours after he allegedly harassed a woman on an LRT train on Wednesday.
Rapid Rail Sdn Bhd said the suspect was believed to have been involved in a similar incident previously.
Prasarana auxiliary police caught the teenager during a tracking operation following a report by a passenger on the Kelana Jaya LRT line.
Initial investigations revealed the suspect was also linked to an earlier incident at the Kuchai station on the Putrajaya line.
Rapid Rail said the man was handed over to the police.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Leo to become first pope to visit Muslim Algeria
Article:
VATICAN CITY: Leo XIV will become the first pope to visit Algeria on Monday, taking a message of dialogue with Islam on a trip that also represents a personal pilgrimage for the American pontiff.
Algeria is the first stop on an 11-day tour of four African nations, covering 18,000 kilometres and also taking in Cameroon, Angola and Equatorial Guinea from April 13 to 23.
No other pontiff has visited Algeria, a North African country where Islam is the state religion, and the 70-year-old's arrival is being eagerly awaited by the Catholic minority.
The visit also holds a strong personal dimension for Pope Leo, as modern-day Algeria was home to Saint Augustine (354-430), a great Christian theologian whose spiritual legacy permeates his pontificate.
As the world watches anxiously with war raging in the Middle East, peaceful coexistence will be at the heart of the pope's message in a country where 99 percent of its 47 million inhabitants are Muslim.
Vatican spokesman Matteo Bruni said the pope's aim was to "address the Islamic world, but also to confront a common challenge of coexistence."
Algeria's constitution guarantees freedom of worship, although the authorities must approve both the place of worship and the preacher.
Yet human rights groups say the repression of religious minorities has continued in Algeria in recent years.
Three organisations — Human Rights Watch, EuroMed Rights and MENA Rights Group — issued a joint letter last week calling on the pontiff to push the issue during his visit.
- Soft power -
The trip has been hailed in the local press for its "symbolic and historical significance," extending far beyond Algeria's 9,000 Catholics.
For the government daily El Moudjahid, it is a symbol of "Algerian soft power", that reflects the country's "stability, its role as a regional mediator, and its capacity to engage in dialogue with global actors," the newspaper stated.
In Algiers Monday morning, Pope Leo will meet with President Abdelmadjid Tebboune and deliver a speech to the authorities and the diplomatic corps.
No public appearances are planned in the capital, and the famous popemobile, the white vehicle pontiffs use to drive past crowds of the faithful, will remain at the airport, according to the Casbah Tribune news site.
In the afternoon, the pope will visit the Great Mosque of Algiers — one of the largest mosques in the world — and also meet with the Catholic community.
Leo will also pray privately in the chapel dedicated to 19 priests and nuns murdered during the dark decade of civil war (1992-2002).
However, he will not visit the Tibhirine monastery, whose monks were kidnapped and murdered in 1996, an event still shrouded in mystery.
- Son of St Augustine -
The most symbolic stop for the pope will be on Tuesday with a trip to the eastern city of Annaba, the one-time home of Saint Augustine, where Leo will hold a mass.
From the moment he became pope on May 8, 2025, following the death of Pope Francis, Leo has presented himself as a "son" of the famed theologian.
The former Robert Francis Prevost is a member of the Augustinian order, which was founded in the 13th century and is based on the ideals of communal living and sharing.
Father Fred Wekesa, rector of the Saint Augustine Basilica at Annaba, said Leo's visit was a "deeply significant moment" that would give his small flock a "message of encouragement and solidarity."
"All too often, some people view this country only through the lens of the 'dark years'," he added, alluding to the country's bloody civil war from 1992 to 2002, when 200,000 people were killed in the conflict between Islamists and security forces.
"With the Holy Father's visit... the whole world will see the hospitality and generosity of the Algerian people," said Wekesa.
"We are capable of living together in peace." — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Video of teen throwing brick from flat goes viral
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A video of a teenager tossing a brick from the upper floor of a flat has gone viral.
The 34-second closed-circuit television (CCTV) footage, dated yesterday, shows the youth walking towards a pile of bricks along a walkway and picking one up.
He is seen peeking over the wall to check his surroundings before throwing the brick.
The teenager then quickly ducks and waits for several seconds before continuing along the walkway.
Social media users have called on the authorities to take action, saying that the falling object could have been fatal to anyone below.
In 2018, a 15-year-old boy was
killed
when an office chair thrown from the 21st floor of the Seri Pantai People's Housing Project (PPR) struck him.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Eric Swalwell, candidate for California governor, denies sexual assault allegations
Article:
CALIFORNIA: US Representative Eric Swalwell, a California Democrat and a leading candidate for governor of the state, has been accused of sexual assault by a former congressional staffer, allegations he forcefully denied as false and politically motivated.
Several leading Democratic politicians responded by urging Swalwell to end his campaign for governor.
The San Francisco Chronicle reported on Friday that a woman who previously worked in Swalwell's district office accused him of two non-consensual sexual encounters, one while she was employed by him in 2019 and another in 2024 after she had left his staff.
The woman, whom the newspaper did not name, was quoted as saying she had been too intoxicated on both occasions to consent, according to the report.
The former staffer later told CNN that Swalwell had raped her during the later encounter, which Swalwell strongly denied.
"I was pushing him off of me, saying no," the woman told CNN on camera but in shadow to disguise her appearance. When asked how he responded, the woman said, "He didn't stop."
CNN reported that three other women had accused Swalwell of separate instances of sexual misconduct, including unsolicited explicit messages or photos. Swalwell denied those claims as well.
"These allegations are false and come on the eve of an election against the frontrunner for governor," Swalwell said in a statement to the Chronicle and CNN.
"For nearly 20 years, I have served the public - as a prosecutor and a congressman - and have always protected women. I will defend myself with the facts and where necessary bring legal action.
My focus in the coming days is to be with my wife and children and defend our decades of service against these lies."
A spokesperson for Swalwell did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.
The accusations have jolted the campaign. US Senator Adam Schiff, another nationally prominent Democrat, withdrew his endorsement and urged Swalwell to leave the race.
The California Teachers Association, the largest teachers' union in the state, suspended its support. House Democratic leadership, including Hakeem Jeffries, said Swalwell should "immediately end his campaign."
In a large field of candidates from multiple parties, Swalwell has been widely considered one of the leading contenders to advance to a runoff in California's nonpartisan voting for governor.
The top two finishers in June's primary will advance to the general election in November, even if they are from the same party.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Americans give record-low marks to economy, in ominous sign for Republicans
Article:
WASHINGTON: Americans are souring on the US economy to a degree never before seen, with the stiffest inflation in four years exacerbating the political risk for President Donald Trump, who some administration officials worry has lost focus on affordability problems for voters as he trains attention on the war against Iran.
Few issues resonate with US voters more deeply than price increases, and the latest inflationary upswing is unsettling key insiders at the White House worried about their Republican Party's prospects in critical midterm elections less than seven months away.
Republican lawmakers and senior White House aides have for months urged Trump to focus more on the economy, which is the top concern for voters.
Trump has struggled, however, to show that he feels Americans' pain and has repeatedly declared victory over inflation, despite official data showing otherwise.
Data from the Labour Department on Friday showed inflation soared in March, the first full month of the war the US and Israel launched against Iran on Feb 28, which resulted in Teheran choking off a fifth of the world's oil supply from flowing through the critical Strait of Hormuz.
The resulting upward spike in crude oil prices drove a record-setting increase in gasoline costs across the US, Bureau of Labour Statistics data showed, and that pushed headline inflation up by the most since June 2022 when the post-COVID pandemic price surge that ravaged former President Joe Biden's political prospects hit its peak.
Alongside that, household sentiment over the economy took a nosedive, with the University of Michigan's benchmark Consumer Sentiment Index sliding to a record low at the beginning of April.
"Demographic groups across age, income, and political party all posted setbacks in sentiment, as did every component of the index, reflecting the widespread nature of this month's fall," survey Director Joanne Hsu said in a statement.
And it was not just Trump's perennial critics among Democrats giving low marks to the state of the economy now and its prospects for the future.
The biggest decline was recorded among the survey's self-identified Republicans, whose sentiment score now sits near its lowest since Trump returned to office in January 2025 after winning back the White House on promises to lower the high prices that dogged Biden through most of his presidency.
Indeed, some top Trump administration officials, including White House Chief of Staff Susie Wiles, have become increasingly alarmed that not enough effort is being spent on bringing down high prices.
[[nid:1415147]]
Wiles has privately urged advisers to be more declarative on the economic and political downsides of the war, a White House official told Reuters this week on condition of anonymity to discuss sensitive deliberations.
Beyond the University of Michigan survey, public opinion polling shows that Americans are increasingly losing confidence in Trump's stewardship of the economy, which political analysts say could hurt his Republican Party as it fights to retain slim majorities in Congress in the November midterms.
"President Trump has always been clear about short-term disruptions as a result of Operation Epic Fury, disruptions that the administration has been diligently working to mitigate," White House spokesman Kush Desai said Friday on social media as the White House made an effort to shift focus from gasoline prices.
"Prices of eggs, beef, prescription drugs, dairy and other household essentials are falling or remain stable thanks to President Trump's policies."
Indeed, food prices were unchanged last month and egg prices have fallen 45 per cent in the last year, the most ever, a fact Trump frequently cites.
But economists do worry that energy costs - unless lowered by a deal with Iran to reopen the strait and restart the flow of oil - could start feeding into a wider inflation breakout if they remain elevated for much longer.
Diesel fuel costs that have been pushed to within 20 cents a gallon of their record high are a critical cost input for truckers and farmers, and those costs could soon be passed along to consumers in the form of higher food costs.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Artemis II astronauts return to Earth, capping historic Moon mission [WATCH]
Article:
SAN DIEGO: The NASA spacecraft carrying four astronauts splashed down as planned Friday off the California coast, capping the US space agency's successful crewed test mission around the Moon, the first such flyby in more than 50 years.
Mission commander Reid Wiseman reported that the crewmembers – himself along with Christina Koch, Victor Glover and Jeremy Hansen – were "stable" and "green."
"They're in great condition, that's what that means," said Rob Navias, the NASA public affairs official who narrated their return on the agency's livestream.
Following a brief but nerve-wracking communications blackout during their high-stakes re-entry, mission commander Wiseman's voice triggered relief that the astronauts were well on their way back home.
"Houston. Integrity. We have you loud and clear," he said following a voice check from mission control.
NASA and the US military will now help them leave the bobbing capsule, after which they will be flown to a recovery ship off the Pacific coast near San Diego.
As the astronauts returned to Earth their spacecraft reached maximum speeds more than 30 times the speed of sound, and faced searing temperatures around half as hot as the surface of the Sun. It was a key test of their heat shield, which in an earlier trial mission had faced complications.
But the Artemis II re-entry went off without a hitch.
"This was a textbook entry and a textbook touchdown," said Navias.
Historic journey
The approximately 10-day mission that began with a dramatic launch from Florida on April 1 was studded with firsts, records and extraordinary moments.
It was considered a key stepping stone towards eventual crewed lunar landings.
It was the first crewed mission of NASA's programme aiming to install a sustained presence on the Moon, including the eventual construction of a base.
The second phase of the Artemis programme was a test mission to verify the reliability of the Orion capsule, which before now had not carried humans.
The voyage also broke a record: the four astronauts became the humans to travel furthest away from the Earth, at 252,756 miles (406,771 kilometres).
[[nid:1415179]]
While hurtling through deep space and zipping around the Moon the astronauts took thousands of photographs, amassing a stunning portfolio of images that captivated people on Earth.
They also witnessed a solar eclipse along with extraordinary meteorite strikes on the lunar surface that had left NASA scientists awe-struck.
Several achievements added to the voyage's historic nature: Glover was the first person of colour to fly around the Moon, Koch was the first woman, and Canadian Hansen the first non-American.
Vital heat shield
The Orion capsule passed a key test: the re-entry stakes were particularly high given concerns that arose during Artemis I, when an uncrewed 2022 test flight to the Moon and back saw a crucial heat shield erode in unexpected ways.
To minimise risks this time around, NASA shifted the re-entry path they had used in the test mission, after determining it had played a role in the complications.
The astronauts returned at a steeper and thus shorter trajectory, which NASA officials in discussion with the astronauts concluded would reduce risk to an acceptable degree.
Still, the heat shield situation prompted uneasy comparisons to the Challenger and Columbia space shuttle disasters of 1986 and 2003 respectively, when astronauts died after warning signs were set aside.
But to the relief of NASA, the astronauts and their families along with the public at large, Artemis II completed a successful return.
Loved ones were watching the journey home from mission control in Houston, where the astronauts are due to reunite with their families, likely this weekend.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: National Archives D-G: Over 10,000 items on Pak Lah gathered for planned memorial in Penang
Article:
GEORGE TOWN, April 11 — The National Archives of Malaysia (ANM) has collected more than 10,000 materials related to former prime minister Abdullah Ahmad Badawi, which are currently being catalogued for placement at the Memorial Tun Abdullah Ahmad Badawi to be built at Vision Park in Bertam near here.
Its director-general Zaidin Mohd Noor said the collection was carried out in collaboration with the Prime Minister’s Department (JPM) and comprises various official records, administrative documents, as well as materials related to the fifth prime minister’s involvement in politics and international relations.
“Officially, we have collected more than 10,000 materials related to Tun Abdullah and they are now in the process of being catalogued. Among the materials collected are official correspondence during his administration, records of his involvement in political parties, as well as documents of official overseas visits, including speeches at the international level such as at the United Nations.
“All materials obtained will be preserved as part of the nation’s historical records, although only a portion will be displayed to the public based on specific themes and periods at the Memorial Tun Abdullah Ahmad Badawi, including leadership aspects such as the Islam Hadhari concept introduced by the former prime minister,” he told reporters here today.
He was met after the “Kayuhan Jejak Seniman: Rumah Kelahiran P. Ramlee” programme, officiated by Penang Yang Dipertua Negeri Tun Ramli Ngah Talib. As a symbolic gesture, Tun Ramli cycled 14.1 kilometres from his official residence, Seri Mutiara on Jalan York, to the birthplace of P. Ramlee.
Previously, the Penang state government approved a parcel of land at Vision Park, Bertam, Kepala Batas for the construction of Memorial Tun Abdullah Ahmad Badawi in honour of the former prime minister’s services and legacy.
Penang Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow was reported as saying that ownership of the land had been finalised and would be handed over to ANM to realise the construction of the gallery proposed by Bertam assemblyman Datuk Seri Reezal Merican Naina Merican.
Elaborating further, Zaidin said the Memorial Tun Abdullah Ahmad Badawi project is expected to be implemented under the 13th Malaysia Plan (RMK13), subject to funding approval by the Federal Government.
He said based on experience in building memorials for past national figures, the implementation period typically takes between three and five years, although he did not disclose the construction cost of the memorial.
“We at ANM also welcome contributions of materials related to national figures, including Tun Abdullah, from the public to enrich the archival collection and ensure the memorial content to be developed is more comprehensive and relevant to society and researchers,” he said.
He added that the memorial is expected to become a historical reference centre as well as an educational platform to introduce Tun Abdullah’s contributions and leadership to future generations.
Tun Abdullah, the fifth prime minister, served as the Member of Parliament for Kepala Batas for eight consecutive terms from 1978 to 2013. He passed away at the National Heart Institute (IJN) on April 14, 2025. — Bernama

Title: Federal Territories launches free aerobics programme to fight stress and obesity
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 11 — Prime Minister’s wife, Datuk Seri Dr Wan Azizah Wan Ismail, has urged city dwellers to join the 2026 Federal Territories Community Aerobics Programme (PSKWP2026) to help manage everyday stress.
Dr Wan Azizah, the Bandar Tun Razak MP, said the free health programme with the slogan Komuniti Aktif, Wilayah Progresif was highly significant and gave people space to relieve stress.
“Alhamdulillah, this is a great initiative, so make the most of it,” she told reporters at the PSKWP2026 launch at Dataran Perumahan Awam Sri Sabah 3B, Bandar Tun Razak, today.
Minister in the Prime Minister’s Department (Federal Territories) Hannah Yeoh launched the programme, attended by Kuala Lumpur mayor Datuk Seri Fadlun Mak Ujud and Prime Minister’s political secretary Datuk Azman Abidin.
Meanwhile, Hannah said the Federal Territories Department initiative, coordinated by the Federal Territories Sports Council, is open to all and will run weekly throughout the year.
“We want to encourage healthy living, especially for residents in the People’s Housing Projects and public housing, so they don’t need to pay for aerobics classes themselves.
“It’s part of our efforts to address high obesity rates in Kuala Lumpur, Putrajaya and Labuan,” she said.
The sessions run simultaneously at 25 public housing sites in Kuala Lumpur, expanding this weekend to 44: 33 in Kuala Lumpur, six in Putrajaya, five in Labuan, supported by partnerships with the Malaysia Fitness Association, residents’ councils, Kuala Lumpur City Hall, Putrajaya Corporation, Labuan Corporation and other agencies.
PSKWP2026 is expected to attract a steady turnout and become a key Federal Territories programme for ongoing community wellbeing. — Bernama

Title: RM3.5m incentive launched to help palm oil traders meet sustainability standards, says minister
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 11 — The Ministry of Plantation and Commodities has introduced an incentive amounting to RM3.5 million in an effort to support oil palm fresh fruit bunches (FFB) traders in obtaining the Malaysian Sustainable Palm Oil (MSPO) certification.
The Minister of Plantation and Commodities, Datuk Seri Noraini Ahmad, said this is a proactive step by the government to help oil palm FFB traders meet current compliance requirements, while also strengthening the resilience of the national palm oil supply chain.
More than 3,000 eligible oil palm FFB traders nationwide are expected to benefit, subject to the stipulated terms and conditions, she said in a statement today.
She said applications for this incentive can be submitted to the MSPO Council starting April 15, 2026, through the e-MSPO system.
“The implementation of this initiative is in line with efforts to strengthen the comprehensive implementation of MSPO 2.0, while supporting the national agenda to ensure the Malaysian palm oil industry continues to develop sustainably, responsibly and resiliently,” she said.
The initiative reflects the government’s ongoing efforts to safeguard the welfare of stakeholders and strengthen confidence in Malaysia’s palm oil sector in both domestic and international markets. — Bernama

Title: Bera cops: Four more held over Triang kopitiam slashing attack, two to be charged
Article:
KUANTAN, April 11 — Police have detained four more men suspected of involvement in an attack involving sharp weapons at a coffee shop in Triang, Bera on March 29.
Bera police chief Supt Zulkiflee Nazir said all the suspects, aged between 27 and 37, surrendered themselves at the district police headquarters between April 6 and 8.
“So far, five arrests have been made, with four individuals still in police custody while another has been released on police bail on health grounds,” he said in a statement today.
He said one suspect was remanded from April 7 to 10, while two others were remanded from April 8 to April 13 to assist in investigations.
It was reported that police had detained a man believed to be involved in the case at a hospital in Kuala Lumpur on April 4.
“The remand of the suspect detained on April 4 has been extended for four days, from April 10 to 13,” he said, adding that police are tracking down two more individuals to assist in the investigation.
Zulkiflee said police have completed and submitted the investigation papers to the deputy public prosecutor, and two suspects are expected to be charged in court on Monday under Sections 326 and 352 of the Penal Code.
Meanwhile, he said the victim of the incident was discharged on Wednesday after receiving treatment at Sultan Haji Ahmad Shah Hospital in Temerloh. — Bernama

Title: Pilot phase to iron out gaps in Malaysia’s new health insurance scheme, says deputy finance minister
